## BROWSER-QA-AGENT DEFINITION REPORT

### Full Definition

The **browser-qa-agent** is a specialized QA validation agent defined in `/Users/bohdanpytaichuk/Documents/4ra/coding-agents-settings/.claude/agents/browser-qa-agent.md`. Here is its complete specification:

#### Agent Metadata
- **Name:** browser-qa-agent
- **Description:** UI validation agent that executes user stories against web apps and reports pass/fail results with screenshots at every step. Use for QA, acceptance testing, user story validation, or UI verification. Supports parallel instances.
- **Model:** opus
- **Color:** green
- **Skill:** browser-use (CLI-based browser automation)

#### Core Purpose
Execute user stories against web applications by:
1. Parsing user stories into discrete sequential steps
2. Taking screenshots at each step
3. Reporting structured pass/fail results
4. Capturing console errors on failures
5. Stopping execution and marking remaining steps as SKIPPED on first failure

#### Variables and Configuration

| Variable | Value | Purpose |
|----------|-------|---------|
| SCREENSHOTS_DIR | `./screenshots/browser-qa` | Base directory for all QA screenshots |
| VISION | `false` | When true, take screenshot after every browser action (manual, browser-use has no built-in vision mode) |
| Directory Structure | `SCREENSHOTS_DIR/<story-kebab-name>_<8-char-uuid>/` | Each run creates uniquely named subdirectory |
| Screenshot Naming | `00_<step-name>.png`, `01_<step-name>.png`, etc. | Sequential numbering for visual trace |

#### Workflow

**Input:** Accepts user stories in ANY of these formats:
- Simple sentence descriptions
- Step-by-step imperative instructions
- BDD Given/When/Then format
- Narrative with assertions
- Checklist format

**Execution Steps:**
1. **Parse** the user story into discrete, sequential steps
2. **Setup** — derive a named session, create screenshots subdirectory
3. **Execute each step sequentially:**
   - Perform action via `browser-use` skill
   - Take screenshot: `browser-use -s <session> screenshot <path>/<##_step-name>.png`
   - Evaluate PASS or FAIL
   - On FAIL: capture JS console errors, stop execution, mark remaining steps as SKIPPED
4. **Close** the session: `browser-use -s <session> close`
5. **Return** structured pass/fail report

#### Report Format

**On Success:**
```
✅ SUCCESS

**Story:** <story name>
**Steps:** N/N passed
**Screenshots:** ./screenshots/browser-qa/<story-name>_<uuid>/

| #   | Step             | Status | Screenshot       |
| --- | ---------------- | ------ | ---------------- |
| 1   | Step description | PASS   | 00_step-name.png |
| 2   | Step description | PASS   | 01_step-name.png |
```

**On Failure:**
```
❌ FAILURE

**Story:** <story name>
**Steps:** X/N passed
**Failed at:** Step Y
**Screenshots:** ./screenshots/browser-qa/<story-name>_<uuid>/

| #   | Step             | Status  | Screenshot       |
| --- | ---------------- | ------- | ---------------- |
| 1   | Step description | PASS    | 00_step-name.png |
| 2   | Step description | FAIL    | 01_step-name.png |
| 3   | Step description | SKIPPED | —                |

### Failure Detail
**Step Y:** Step description
**Expected:** What should have happened
**Actual:** What actually happened

### Console Errors
<JS console errors captured at time of failure>
```

#### Supported Input Formats (Examples from Definition)

1. **Simple Sentence:**
   ```
   Verify the homepage of http://example.com loads and shows a hero section
   ```

2. **Step-by-Step Imperative:**
   ```
   Login to http://example.com (email: user@test.com, pw: secret123).
   Navigate to /dashboard.
   Verify there are at least 3 widgets.
   Click the first widget.
   Verify the detail page loads.
   ```

3. **BDD Format:**
   ```
   Given I am logged into http://example.com
   When I navigate to /dashboard
   Then I should see a list of widgets with columns: name, status, value
   And each widget should have a numeric value
   ```

4. **Narrative with Assertions:**
   ```
   As a logged-in user on http://example.com, go to the dashboard.
   Assert: the page title contains "Dashboard".
   Assert: at least 3 widgets are visible.
   Assert: the top widget has a value under 100.
   ```

5. **Checklist Format:**
   ```
   url: http://example.com/dashboard
   auth: user@test.com / secret123
   - [ ] Dashboard loads
   - [ ] At least 3 widgets visible
   - [ ] Values are numeric
   - [ ] Clicking a widget opens detail view
   ```

### How Browser-QA-Agent is Used in the System

#### 1. **In `/test_comprehensive` Command**
The comprehensive testing command deploys browser-qa-agents in **Phase 6: Browser QA Testing** to validate affected web surfaces after code changes. It:
- Determines if browser testing is needed (any frontend files or API endpoints changed?)
- Defines concrete browser test scenarios (happy paths, navigation, forms, error states, responsive layouts)
- Launches one browser-qa-agent per scenario in parallel using the `Agent` tool
- Collects PASS/FAIL results with screenshot evidence
- Reports all failures with step-by-step reproduction

**Example deployment:**
```typescript
Agent({
  description: "Browser QA: [scenario name]",
  prompt: "You are a QA tester. Execute the following user story against [APP_URL]:\n\n**Scenario**: [name]\n**Given**: [precondition]\n**When**: [action]\n**Then**: [expected]\n\nNavigate to the page, perform the actions, take screenshots at each step, and report PASS or FAIL with evidence.",
  subagent_type: "browser-qa-agent",
  run_in_background: true
})
```

#### 2. **In `/ui-review` Command**
The UI review command generates user stories from specifications or diffs, then deploys parallel browser-qa-agents to validate each story:
- Accepts spec text, file path, or `git diff` as input
- Generates user story YAML files via a sub-agent
- Discovers and parses all stories
- Spawns one `browser-qa-agent` per story in parallel
- Collects results into an aggregated "UI Review Summary" report
- Saves all screenshots to `screenshots/browser-qa/{timestamp}_{uuid}/`

**Workflow step verb vocabulary used:**
- `Navigate to` — go to a URL
- `Click` — click an element
- `Fill in` — type into an input field
- `Verify` — assert something is visible/true
- `Wait for` — wait for an element or condition
- `Scroll to` — scroll to an element
- `Hover over` — hover on an element

#### 3. **Parallel Execution Pattern**
Both commands deploy multiple browser-qa-agents in a **fan-out pattern**:
- All agents launched in a single message for parallel execution
- Each agent gets its own named browser session (persistent across commands)
- Screenshots are organized per story in timestamped directories
- Results are collected as agents complete asynchronously

### Browser-Use Skill Integration

The browser-qa-agent relies on the **browser-use** skill (defined in `.claude/skills/browser-use/SKILL.md`), which provides:

**Key Commands Used:**
- `browser-use -s <session> open <url>` — navigate to URL
- `browser-use -s <session> state` — get clickable element indexes
- `browser-use -s <session> click <index>` — click by numeric index
- `browser-use -s <session> input <index> "text"` — click and type
- `browser-use -s <session> screenshot [path]` — capture screenshot
- `browser-use -s <session> eval "<js>"` — execute JavaScript (e.g., capture console errors)
- `browser-use -s <session> wait selector "<css>"` — wait for element visibility
- `browser-use -s <session> close` — close browser session

**Session Architecture:**
- Daemon-based: first command starts background daemon per session
- Persistent state: cookies, localStorage preserved across commands
- ~50ms latency between commands
- Supports headless (default), `--headed` (visible), and `--profile` (real Chrome with existing logins)

### Real-World Usage Example

From the `/ui-review` command documentation, a story like:

```yaml
stories:
  - name: "Front page loads with posts"
    url: "https://news.ycombinator.com/"
    workflow: |
      Navigate to https://news.ycombinator.com/
      Verify the front page loads successfully
      Verify at least 10 posts are visible, each with a title and a link
```

When passed to browser-qa-agent, becomes:
1. Opens https://news.ycombinator.com/ in a named session
2. Takes `00_navigate.png` screenshot
3. Waits for page to load, takes `01_verify-loads.png`
4. Uses `browser-use state` to find post elements
5. Verifies at least 10 posts exist, takes `02_verify-posts.png`
6. Reports: **✅ SUCCESS | Steps: 3/3 passed**
7. Saves all 3 screenshots to `screenshots/browser-qa/{run-id}/hackernews/front-page-loads-with-posts/`


**Root Cause Analysis:**

**Architecture of the Hooks System:**

1. **Event Types**: Hooks are triggered at lifecycle events:
   - `PreToolUse`: Before tool execution (can deny)
   - `PostToolUse`: After tool success
   - `PostToolUseFailure`: After tool failure
   - `Stop`: When agent output stops (final validation gate)
   - `SessionStart`/`SessionEnd`: Session lifecycle
   - `Notification`: When awaiting user input

2. **Validator Pattern**: Validators are executable scripts (uv-based Python or shell commands) that:
   - Read hook context from stdin as JSON
   - Perform validation logic
   - Exit with 0 (pass) or 2 (fail)
   - Write output to stderr for visibility
   - Process and log data independently

3. **Stop Hook Mechanism** (most relevant for output quality):
   - Runs one or more validators in sequence
   - Each validator can block the agent's stop by exiting 2
   - Used to enforce completion of deliverables (specs must have required sections, files must be created, tests must pass)

   - Print detailed output to stderr for agent visibility
   - Never block; purely advisory/informational

1. **Always exit with intent**: Exit 0 means "validation passed"; exit 2 means "validation failed, block stop". No other codes.

2. **Use JSON for structured output**: Both input (stdin) and output (JSON decision patterns) use JSON for machine readability.

3. **Separate concerns**: Let validators focus on one job. Chain them if you need multiple checks.

4. **Make validators idempotent**: Running a validator twice should produce the same result (aside from timing).

5. **Log comprehensively**: Use session log directories to store full test reports, not just pass/fail.

7. **Handle missing context gracefully**: Not all specs have browser test scenarios. Validators should skip gracefully (exit 0) if nothing to test.

8. **Consider performance tiers**:
   - Tier 1 (Fast): File existence, syntax checks, unit tests (~seconds)
   - Tier 2 (Medium): Linting, type checking, spec validation (~10s)

### DETAILED ANALYSIS

**Architecture Overview:**

The browser automation system has three tiers:

**Tier 1: CLI Tool (browser-use)**
- An external third-party CLI (`browser-use`) installed via `uv pip install browser-use` or curl script
- Daemon-based architecture with ~50ms command latency per session
- Each session gets its own background daemon communicating via Unix socket at `~/.browser-use/`
- Headless Chromium by default; `--headed` for visible window; `--profile` for real Chrome with existing cookies/logins
- `--connect` and `--cdp-url` for connecting to already-running Chrome instances via Chrome DevTools Protocol
- Element interaction via numeric indexes from `state` command (not DOM selectors or element refs)
- Key flags: `-s <name>` (named session), `--headed`, `--profile [name]`, `--connect`, `--cdp-url`, `--json`, `--mcp`

**Tier 2: Agent (browser-qa-agent)**
- Declared in `.claude/agents/browser-qa-agent.md` with `skills: [browser-use]`
- Model: opus; Color: green
- Accepts user stories in multiple formats: simple sentence, step-by-step imperative, Given/When/Then (BDD), narrative with assertions, or checklist
- Workflow: parse story into steps, derive session name, create screenshot directory, execute each step sequentially with screenshots, report structured pass/fail
- On failure: captures JS console errors via `eval`, stops execution, marks remaining steps SKIPPED
- Screenshots saved to `./screenshots/browser-qa/<story-kebab-name>_<8-char-uuid>/` with numbered filenames
- VISION variable controls whether screenshots are taken after every action (default: false)

**Tier 3: Orchestration Commands**
- `/ui-review`: Generates user story YAML from specs/diffs/inline text via a sub-agent, then fans out parallel browser-qa-agent instances. Uses TeamCreate/TaskCreate for parallel execution. Reports aggregated results.
- `/test_comprehensive`: Phase 6 deploys browser-qa-agents when web surfaces are affected. Uses Agent tool with `run_in_background: true` for parallelism. Part of a 7-phase testing pyramid.

**Browser-Use CLI Flags (Complete):**

| Flag | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `--headed` | Show browser window (must be before subcommand) |
| `--profile [NAME]` | Use real Chrome with existing logins/cookies. Default profile: "Default". Can specify "Profile 1", etc. |
| `--connect` | Auto-discover running Chrome via CDP |
| `--cdp-url <url>` | Connect to Chrome via specific CDP URL (e.g., `http://localhost:9222`) |
| `-s NAME` / `--session NAME` | Target a named session (daemon-per-session) |
| `--json` | Output as JSON |
| `--mcp` | Run as MCP server |

**The `--profile` flag specifically:**
```
browser-use --profile open <url>                    # Real Chrome, "Default" profile
browser-use --profile "Profile 1" open <url>        # Real Chrome, specific profile
```
This launches the user's actual Chrome browser with all existing logins, cookies, extensions, and saved data. The profile name maps to Chrome profile directories.
